text: robotic assisted right scrotal hernia repair with mesh by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a thirty-two year old male who presented with a large bulge in his right scrotum for more than a year with on and off pain and discomfort during his training and exercise and found to have a large right scrotal hernia. This video demonstrates the advantages of robotic surgery in repair of a large sclerodal hernia. The robotic platform allows the surgeon to perform the operation with superior visualization, precise dissection and bloodless field.

robotic assisted right scrotal hernia repair with mesh by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a thirty-two year old male who presented with a large bulge in his right scrotum for more than a year with on and off pain and discomfort during his training and exercise and found to have a large right scrotal hernia. This video demonstrates the advantages of robotic surgery in repair of a large sclerodal hernia. The robotic platform allows the surgeon to perform the operation with superior visualization, precise dissection and bloodless field.

The surgeon creates a transverse peritoneal incision to enter the preperitoneal space, forming a preperitoneal flap. Dissection proceeds medially towards the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, facilitating medial placement of the mesh. The hernia sac is addressed, revealing significant scar tissue between the cord structures and the hernia sac.

The surgeon creates a transverse peritoneal incision and enters the preperitoneal space, forming a preperitoneal flap. Dissection proceeds medially towards the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, facilitating medial placement of the mesh. The hernia sac is then addressed, revealing significant scar tissue between the cord structures and the hernia sac.

Using the enhanced visualization and wristed instrumentation of the robotic platform, the surgeon performs a precise dissection to separate the hernia sac from the cord structures, including the blood vessels to the testicle and the vas deferens. The dissection is carefully executed to protect these vital structures while maintaining a bloodless field.

Using the enhanced visualization and wristed instrumentation of the robotic platform, the surgeon performs a precise dissection to separate the hernia sac from the cord structures, including the blood vessels to the testicle and the vas deferens, ensuring a bloodless operation.

The surgeon continues the dissection medially to identify the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, extending the dissection inferiorly, superiorly, and laterally to create a pocket for mesh placement. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the hernia defect is reduced, and the mesh is precisely positioned in the preperitoneal space to cover the hernia defect, ensuring it fits snugly to prevent movement.

The surgeon continues the dissection medially to identify the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, extending the dissection inferiorly, superiorly, and laterally to create a pocket for mesh placement. The preperitoneal fat incarcerated in the hernia defect is reduced, and the mesh is precisely positioned in the preperitoneal space to cover the hernia defect, ensuring it fits snugly to prevent movement.

The surgeon secures the mesh to the abdominal wall using bio-glue, followed by closing the peritoneal defect with an absorbable suture. The wristed robotic instruments aid in the precise execution of these steps, ensuring the hernia sac is included in the repair to reinforce the peritoneum.

The surgeon secures the mesh to the abdominal wall using bio-glue. The peritoneal defect is then closed primarily with an absorbable suture, aided by the wristed instrumentation of the robotic platform. The hernia sac is included in the repair to reinforce the peritoneum.

This patient followed the procedure very well. It was an outpatient surgery and he went home the same day with no complications or post-op problems. He was seen subsequently in the office and was able to go back to normal, heavy exercise and activity six weeks from the time of surgery.
